title: Satellite Imagery Reveals Environmental Damage from Ukrainian Drone Strikes on Russian Oil Infrastructure
summary: NASA Earth observation satellites captured extensive smoke plumes and oil contamination from repeated Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian refineries at Tuapse and Perm, documenting environmental damage visible from space.
contents:
## NASA Satellite Observations of Russian Oil Facility Damage

### Observation Details
NASA Earth observation systems detected extensive smoke plumes and environmental contamination stemming from Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian oil infrastructure. The Tuapse oil refinery and export terminal on the Black Sea coast experienced four attacks in just over two weeks, with fires burning for nearly two days before containment.

### Environmental Impact Assessment
- Boiling oil products spilled onto streets in Tuapse, damaging vehicles and infrastructure
- Residents reported "black rain" — oily droplets falling on skin and clothes
- High levels of benzene, a known carcinogen, measured in local air during active fires
- Oil spills along the Black Sea coastline coated birds and fish
- Beached dolphins appeared in Russian media imagery, suggesting marine ecosystem impact
- Smoke plumes extended hundreds of kilometers, visible in MODIS and Landsat sensor data

### Strike Scope
Ukrainian drones also hit the Lukoil-Permnefteorgsintez refinery in Russia's Perm region, over 1,500 kilometers from Ukraine — demonstrating doubled deep-strike capabilities since the February 2022 full-scale invasion. The Ust-Luga terminal on the Baltic Sea sustained three strikes in late March.

### Monitoring Capabilities
NASA's FIRMS (Fire Information for Resource Management System) provides real-time monitoring of active fires, supporting both disaster response coordination and environmental damage assessment. Sentinel and Landsat constellation data offer multi-spectral analysis of contamination extent.
